output:
# Per-Prompt Divergence Attunement for Diversity-Preserving Text-to-Image Alignment: ISODIV (the single-global-divergence limit) and ATTUNE (a partially-pooled, covariate-conditional f-divergence map with exact collapse to the base method)

This document delivers a brief literature review, a focused analysis, and a new research
proposal in the required five-question ("5q") format. The proposal is built on a single
reusable research move: take a quantity that the source line of work fixes as ONE GLOBAL
SCALAR applied identically to every instance, show that a scalar is representationally
incapable of expressing the per-instance optimum (so its harm is silent under pooled metrics,
grows with heterogeneity, and vanishes under homogeneity), and then promote that scalar to a
structured, monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map of an OBSERVABLE covariate that
provably collapses back to the original method at its covariate-blind default.

Here the source line is implicit-reward (DPO-style) alignment of text-to-image diffusion
models, and the specific scalar is the CHOICE OF f-DIVERGENCE CONSTRAINT. The source
Introduction's own contribution is to generalize alignment from the reverse Kullback-Leibler
(reverse-KL) divergence to a FAMILY of f-divergences (Jensen-Shannon, forward-KL, alpha) to
combat the diversity loss caused by reverse-KL's mode-seeking behavior. We argue that this
generalization, while important, still selects ONE global f-divergence and applies it to EVERY
prompt -- and that this is the next representational bottleneck.

Notation (ASCII): pi = aligned policy (the fine-tuned diffusion model); pi_ref = frozen
reference policy; c = text prompt (condition); D_f(P||Q) = E_Q[ f(P/Q) ] = an f-divergence
with convex f, f(1)=0; alpha = the divergence-family parameter that selects a point on the
mode-seeking(fidelity) <-> mass-covering(diversity) spectrum; d_c = an observable per-prompt
diversity-demand covariate; alpha_global = a single global divergence selector; alpha(d_c) =
a per-prompt divergence selector; theta = parameters of the map g_theta; beta = the
DPO temperature; sigmoid = logistic function; eps = diffusion noise. "<=" and ">=" are
inequalities; "*" is multiplication; "->" is "maps to / tends to".

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 Text-to-image generation and the alignment problem

Text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models now produce high-fidelity images that are semantically
coherent with free-form prompts, driven by latent diffusion and large-scale text conditioning
[1, 2, 3, 4], built on the denoising-diffusion formulation [5, 6]. Despite this, self-supervised
pre-trained generators remain imperfectly aligned with human preferences: they can ignore parts
of a prompt, produce low aesthetic quality, or generate undesired content [17, 18]. Aligning
these models with human preferences is therefore a central post-training objective.

### 1.2 From RLHF to implicit-reward (DPO-style) alignment

The dominant alignment recipe is Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) [7, 8]. One
collects a preference dataset of pairwise comparisons over model outputs, fits a reward model
under the Bradley-Terry model [9], and fine-tunes the generator with reinforcement learning
while a reverse-KL penalty keeps the policy close to its reference [15, 16, 17]. For diffusion
models this RL route is realized by DDPO [15], DPOK [16], and reward-feedback fine-tuning [17].
RLHF is effective but introduces a separately trained reward model and the well-known
instability and memory cost of RL.

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [10] removes the explicit reward model: it shows that the
RLHF objective max E[r] - beta * KL(pi || pi_ref) has a closed-form optimal policy
pi*(y|x) proportional to pi_ref(y|x) * exp(r(x,y)/beta), and that substituting the implied
reward r = beta * log( pi(y|x) / pi_ref(y|x) ) + const turns alignment into a simple
classification loss on preference pairs. Diffusion-DPO [11] ports this to T2I by bounding the
DPO objective through the diffusion evidence lower bound, yielding a per-timestep
noise-prediction loss; D3PO [12] does so without any explicit reward model; and SPO [13] makes
the preference signal step-aware along the denoising trajectory. KTO [21] and
Diffusion-KTO [22] replace pairwise data with a prospect-theoretic utility, and beta-DPO [23]
adapts the temperature beta during training. Pick-a-Pic / PickScore [19], ImageReward [18], and
HPSv2 [20] supply the preference data and the evaluation reward models that this whole line
relies on.

### 1.3 The divergence constraint and the diversity problem

Crucially, the "stay close to pi_ref" term in DPO is a REVERSE-KL divergence, KL(pi || pi_ref).
Reverse-KL is mode-seeking: it concentrates probability mass on a subset of the reference
support. In RLHF/DPO this is a feature (it sharpens toward preferred outputs) but also a known
liability: repeated fine-tuning under a mode-seeking penalty reduces output diversity and can
overfit, with the generator collapsing toward a few high-reward modes [17, 22, 24, 25]. The same
mode-seeking-reduces-diversity effect is documented for aligned large language models, where
reverse-KL alignment measurably lowers generation diversity [24], and reward over-optimization
makes it worse [25]. Diversity is not a cosmetic concern for T2I: it is precisely the model's
ability to return many distinct, plausible images for one prompt, and it is measured by
precision/recall for generative models [26], by the Vendi score [27], and by feature spread in
representation spaces such as DINOv2 [28], complementing fidelity metrics like FID [30] and
prompt-faithfulness scores [18, 19, 20].

### 1.4 The source paper's generalization -- and the residual gap

The source Introduction proposes to GENERALIZE alignment beyond reverse-KL to a family of
f-divergences. This mirrors, for T2I, the language-model result "Beyond Reverse KL" [14], which
generalizes DPO to arbitrary f-divergence constraints (forward-KL, Jensen-Shannon, alpha) using
a KKT-derived mapping between reward and optimal policy, and shows that the divergence choice
trades off alignment strength against output diversity. Picking a more mass-covering divergence
(e.g., Jensen-Shannon, as illustrated in the source's Figure 1) preserves more diversity than
reverse-KL.

This is a real advance, but it leaves a precise, unexamined bottleneck. Even with the full
f-divergence family available, the method still selects ONE global divergence -- one value of
alpha, fixed once for the whole model -- and applies it IDENTICALLY to every prompt. A single
global divergence fixes ONE operating point on the fidelity(mode-seeking) <->
diversity(mass-covering) frontier for ALL prompts. But the right operating point is not the
same for every prompt: an open-ended prompt ("an imaginative creature", "a surreal landscape")
benefits from a mass-covering divergence that preserves many modes, whereas a specific,
constrained prompt ("a red octagonal stop sign", "the digit 7 in black on white") benefits from
a mode-seeking divergence that protects fidelity. We name this residual bottleneck ISODIV
(the Isotropic-Divergence-selection limit), and the rest of this document analyzes it and
proposes ATTUNE, a per-prompt divergence map that removes it while provably collapsing back to
the source method.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 2. Key Findings and Analysis

### 2.1 The representational impossibility (ISODIV)

The per-prompt optimal divergence -- equivalently the optimal point alpha*(c) on the
fidelity/diversity frontier -- is a heterogeneous FIELD over the prompt space, because the right
amount of mass-covering depends on how open-ended the prompt is. A single global scalar
alpha_global is a constant function of c. A constant cannot equal a non-constant target:
whenever alpha*(c) varies across prompts, alpha_global is simultaneously too mode-seeking for
open prompts (it collapses their diversity) and too mass-covering for specific prompts (it
erodes their fidelity / prompt-faithfulness). This is a representational impossibility, not a
tuning failure: no amount of searching over the single number alpha_global can make a constant
track a varying alpha*(c). The gap therefore has three signatures that we use throughout:

- SILENT under pooled metrics. Over-diversifying specific prompts and under-diversifying open
  prompts move pooled preference win-rate, pooled reward [18, 19, 20], and pooled FID [30] in
  opposite directions, so they partially cancel in the average. The dominant, easy-to-please
  prompts mask the regressions on the open-ended tail.
- SCALES with heterogeneity. The harm grows with the SPREAD of diversity demand across the
  prompt distribution: the more the prompts differ in how open-ended they are, the larger the
  aggregate mismatch a single alpha_global must incur.
- VANISHES under homogeneity. If every prompt has the same diversity demand (alpha*(c) constant),
  then alpha_global = alpha*(c) is optimal and ISODIV disappears. This is the null condition our
  falsifiers exploit.

### 2.2 Why the gap has stayed invisible: metric blindness (the binding obstacle)

The reason ISODIV has not been targeted is methodological, not conceptual: the field evaluates
alignment with POOLED, prompt-averaged metrics (mean preference win-rate, mean PickScore /
ImageReward / HPSv2, dataset-level FID) [18, 19, 20, 30]. These averages are exactly the
quantities in which the open-prompt diversity loss and the specific-prompt fidelity loss cancel.
The very same property that makes a single global divergence "look fine" (its pooled numbers are
competitive) is what hides its per-prompt damage. Detecting ISODIV requires
diversity-demand-STRATIFIED evaluation (does the open-ended decile lose diversity?) plus
covariate-scramble and homogeneous-null falsifiers; without those instruments the gap is
undetectable, which is why it has persisted even as the f-divergence family became available
[14]. We treat this metric blindness as the BINDING obstacle (O6 in Section 3).

### 2.3 The covariate: an observable, leakage-free per-prompt diversity demand

ISODIV is only actionable if the per-prompt diversity demand is OBSERVABLE cheaply and without
leakage. It is. The frozen reference policy pi_ref already encodes how open-ended a prompt is:
for an open prompt it spontaneously generates a wide spread of images; for a specific prompt its
own samples are tightly clustered. Concretely, define the per-prompt covariate

    d_c = normalized spread of { phi(x_1), ..., phi(x_K) },  x_i ~ pi_ref(.|c),

where phi is a frozen image encoder (DINOv2 [28]) and "spread" is the mean pairwise cosine
distance or the Vendi score [27] of the K reference samples, normalized to [0,1] by a global
scale. High d_c marks an open-ended, diversity-demanding prompt; low d_c marks a specific,
fidelity-demanding prompt. This covariate is CHEAP rather than free: it needs K reference samples
per prompt drawn once in a training-time pre-pass (reused directly where the host already samples
pi_ref, as in on-policy variants such as D3PO [12] / DDPO [15]), a frozen off-the-shelf encoder
phi, and ZERO extra inference-time cost, and it adds no human labels. It
is LEAKAGE-FREE because it is computed from the FROZEN pi_ref, independent of the preference
labels and of the updated policy pi -- so it cannot peek at the very preference signal it is used
to weight, and the identical estimator applies at evaluation time (pi_ref is available there).
A cheap text-only fallback (prompt specificity: distinct content words / a frozen language-model
perplexity) provides a robustness covariate for ablations.

### 2.4 The exact-collapse safety property

The second pillar is a hard safety guarantee: ATTUNE must be a strict GENERALIZATION of the
source method, recovering it exactly at a covariate-blind default. The map g_theta is
parameterized as a sum of monotone non-decreasing basis functions with NO constant term, so that
at theta = 0 the ENTIRE map is identically zero (g_0(d) = 0 for ALL d, not merely the value at
d = 0); together with the precondition alpha_global in [alpha_min, alpha_max] (so the clamp is
inactive), this yields alpha(d_c) = alpha_global for every prompt and makes the ATTUNE loss
identical, term for term, to the source single-global-divergence loss:

    E_collapse = max_c | L_ATTUNE(c) - L_base(c) |  <=  1e-6   (CI-gated build assertion).

Because g_0 is identically zero by construction (not a fitted approximation), there is no
floating-point drift, and the gate is a unit test. Two nested collapses follow: setting theta = 0
recovers the source generalized-f-divergence method, and additionally setting alpha_global to the
reverse-KL value recovers Diffusion-DPO [11] itself. (Note: a homogeneous prompt set with constant
d_c only removes per-prompt VARIATION -- ATTUNE then reduces to SOME single global divergence
alpha_global + g_theta(d_const), which equals the base divergence only when theta = 0; this is
why the homogeneous-prompt falsifier T1 is stated against a RETUNED single-global-f baseline, not
as an exact identity.) This converts the proposal from a risky
re-architecture into a do-no-harm, shadow-deployable layer whose default behavior is provably
the incumbent.

### 2.5 Novelty wedge (C1-C4) and why prior work does not cover it

We position ATTUNE on four simultaneous clauses:

- C1 (layer + cost): operates PURELY at the divergence-selection layer of the preference loss;
  no change to the diffusion backbone, sampler, preference data, or reward model; the only added
  cost is a one-time training-time pre-pass of K reference samples per prompt to estimate d_c
  (reused where the host already samples pi_ref); zero new human labels; zero added inference cost.
- C2 (observable covariate): conditions on an observable, leakage-free per-prompt covariate d_c
  (reference-model intrinsic diversity).
- C3 (structured map): replaces the scalar with a structured, monotone, hierarchically
  partially-pooled map alpha(d_c) -- NOT a single global f, and NOT an unpooled free per-prompt
  alpha.
- C4 (exact collapse): provably reduces to the global-divergence base method at theta=0, with
  E_collapse <= 1e-6.

No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4 (the near-miss analysis is in Section 3, Question 4).
The closest relative, the f-divergence DPO family [14] and its T2I instantiation (the source
paper), satisfies C1 but selects ONE global divergence -- missing C2, C3, and C4. SPO [13]
conditions on TIMESTEP, not per-prompt diversity, and changes the preference signal rather than
the divergence type. beta-DPO [23] adapts the STRENGTH beta of the same reverse-KL, not the
divergence TYPE, and not via a per-prompt diversity covariate with a monotone partially-pooled
map and exact collapse. The reusable insight is that the f-divergence choice has, to our
knowledge, been treated as a single global modeling decision, never as a per-prompt FIELD
allocated by an already-available diversity signal.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 3. Novel Research Idea (5q format)

**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.**

Implicit-reward (DPO-style) alignment of text-to-image diffusion models is moving from a single
reverse-KL constraint toward a family of f-divergence constraints in order to protect generation
diversity. But the divergence is still chosen ONCE and applied to EVERY prompt, even though the
optimal diversity-vs-fidelity trade-off is prompt-specific. We propose to make the f-divergence
a per-prompt function of each prompt's observable diversity demand, estimated for free from the
reference model's own sample spread, using a monotone, partially-pooled map that provably
collapses to the single-divergence base method at its default.

**Here are the five core questions:**

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

How can the f-divergence constraint in implicit-reward (DPO-style) text-to-image alignment be
made a per-prompt function of each prompt's observable diversity demand -- rather than a single
global divergence applied identically to every prompt -- so that open-ended prompts retain
generation diversity while specific prompts retain fidelity, with provable exact reduction to
the single-global-divergence method at its covariate-blind default?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Solving this matters for the research community and for practice along five axes.

P1 -- It converts a global modeling choice into a learnable, observable-covariate-conditional
field. The entire alignment-divergence literature [10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 23] treats "which
divergence" as one hyperparameter fixed for the whole model. Reframing it as a per-prompt map
alpha(d_c) over an observable covariate opens a new design axis and a new evaluation axis
(diversity-demand stratification) that the field currently lacks.

P2 -- It directly attacks the diversity-collapse problem that motivated the source work and the
broader alignment literature [17, 22, 24, 25]. Reverse-KL (and any single global divergence)
either under-protects diversity on open prompts or over-protects it on specific prompts; a
per-prompt divergence is the natural representational fix, and it should improve the
diversity-fidelity Pareto frontier rather than trading one for the other.

P3 -- It is deployable by construction. Because ATTUNE collapses exactly to the incumbent at
theta=0 (E_collapse <= 1e-6), practitioners can adopt it as a strictly do-no-harm layer with a
built-in disable switch, validation-gating any active configuration. This lowers the barrier to
real adoption far below that of a new loss or a new architecture.

P4 -- It is host-agnostic and cheap. ATTUNE sits at the divergence-selection layer, adds only a
one-time training-time reference-sampling pre-pass (no human labels, no inference-time cost), and
applies on top of Diffusion-DPO [11], D3PO [12], SPO [13], or any f-divergence-DPO variant [14]. The
covariate d_c is computed from a frozen encoder [28], so the method ports across base models
(Stable Diffusion [1], SDXL [2]) and preference datasets [18, 19, 20].

P5 -- It transfers a general recipe. The move "promote a globally fixed divergence/regularizer
to a partially-pooled covariate-conditional map with exact collapse" is a template that should
generalize to other alignment settings (language-model f-DPO [14], KTO-style utilities [21, 22],
RL alignment penalties [15, 16]). A clean T2I demonstration -- with stratified evaluation and
falsifiers -- gives the community a reusable methodology and a measurement protocol for
detecting cancellation-hidden gaps.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The difficulty is real and concentrated in six obstacles; naive approaches fail on each.

O1 -- Entangled per-prompt optimum. The ideal divergence per prompt is confounded with prompt
frequency, the base model's competence on that prompt, reward-model noise, and reward scale
[18, 19, 20, 25]. Naively regressing the best alpha against a prompt feature mixes the
diversity-demand axis with these nuisances; the covariate must be chosen and residualized to
isolate open-endedness.

O2 -- Data poverty per prompt. Preference datasets [19] typically have very few (often one)
pairs per prompt. A free per-prompt alpha is therefore extremely high-variance, so the naive
"just learn alpha for each prompt" (baseline B4 below) overfits and is unusable; some form of
pooling is mandatory.

O3 -- Observability and leakage. The covariate must be computable from artifacts we already have
and must NOT see the preference labels or the updated policy, or the estimator becomes circular
and leaks. A naive covariate built from the post-alignment model, or from the very preference
scores being optimized, would inflate results and fail to transfer to evaluation prompts.

O4 -- Multi-axis diversity. "Diversity demand" has semantic, compositional, and stylistic axes;
a scalar d_c must aggregate them without collapsing distinct behaviors (e.g., a prompt that is
semantically narrow but stylistically open). A single naive spread measure may misrank such
prompts.

O5 -- Simultaneous lift, no-regression, and collapse. Increasing mass-covering can hurt prompt
fidelity and pooled preference win-rate. The method must lift tail diversity AND provably not
regress pooled fidelity AND collapse exactly to the base -- three constraints a naive "just use
Jensen-Shannon everywhere" (a different single global divergence) cannot satisfy, because it
simply moves the cancellation, over-diversifying specific prompts.

O6 (BINDING) -- Metric blindness. Pooled preference win-rate, pooled reward, and dataset-level
FID [18, 19, 20, 30] average away the per-prompt over/under-diversification cancellation. Under
these standard metrics ISODIV is invisible and ATTUNE's gains look like noise; the gap can only
be seen and the method only validated with diversity-demand-stratified evaluation plus
covariate-scramble and homogeneous-null falsifiers. This measurement obstacle is why a
straightforward approach not only fails to fix the problem but fails to even detect it, and it
binds every other obstacle.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Gaps and barriers in prior work, and how ATTUNE differs. We position against six near-miss
families; each covers at most two of the wedge clauses C1-C4 (Section 2.5).

NM1 -- Single global reverse-KL DPO: Diffusion-DPO [11], D3PO [12], DPO [10]. These fix the
divergence to reverse-KL for all prompts. They satisfy C1 (divergence/loss layer) but miss C2
(no covariate), C3 (no map), and C4 (they are the base, not a generalization with a collapse
gate). They are the collapse sub-target (alpha_global = reverse-KL, theta = 0).

NM2 (SHARPEST) -- Single global f-divergence DPO: "Beyond Reverse KL" [14] for language models
and its text-to-image instantiation (the source paper). This generalizes the FAMILY of
divergences and is the direct conceptual parent, but it still selects ONE global f and applies
it to all prompts. It satisfies C1 only; it misses C2 (no per-prompt conditioning), C3 (one
global choice, no monotone partially-pooled map), and C4 (it is the base method, with no
exact-collapse-to-global-f construction). ATTUNE's whole contribution is to add C2+C3+C4 on top
of exactly this method, which is also its primary collapse target.

NM3 -- Step-aware / trajectory-conditional preference: SPO [13]. SPO conditions on the DENOISING
TIMESTEP and changes the granularity of the preference signal, not the divergence TYPE, and not
per prompt. It misses the per-prompt-diversity covariate (C2), the divergence map (C3), and
exact collapse to a global-f method (C4). It is orthogonal and composable with ATTUNE.

NM4 -- Adaptive strength, not type: beta-DPO [23]. This adapts the temperature beta -- the
STRENGTH of the SAME reverse-KL -- by batch-level gradient/margin statistics. Changing beta
slides along one divergence; it does not change the mode-seeking/mass-covering SHAPE, is not
conditioned on a per-prompt diversity covariate, uses no partial pooling, and does not yield an
exact-collapse identity to a fixed-f base. It misses C2, C3, and C4 (covering at most C1).

NM5 -- Alternative utilities: KTO [21] and Diffusion-KTO [22]. These swap pairwise data for a
prospect-theoretic utility but still apply a single global utility/reference to all prompts; no
per-prompt divergence-type conditioning, no diversity covariate, no collapse to f-DPO. They miss
C2, C3, C4.

NM6 -- RL alignment with a global KL penalty: DDPO [15], DPOK [16], reward-feedback fine-tuning
[17]. Explicit-reward RL with a single global reverse-KL penalty (or its strength), dependent on
a trained reward model; no per-prompt divergence-type map and no exact collapse to an
implicit-reward f-DPO. They miss C2, C3, C4.

The barrier. Three things kept ISODIV unsolved. First, the field treated the f-divergence as a
single global modeling decision -- pick reverse-KL, or pick Jensen-Shannon, or pick one alpha
for the whole model [14] -- and, to our knowledge, never as a per-prompt field. Second, the
obvious covariate was hidden in plain sight: the reference model's own per-prompt sample spread
is a cheap, already-available diversity signal, but it was used (if at all) only as augmentation
material or as an evaluation diagnostic, never as the SELECTION COVARIATE for the divergence. Third, and
decisively, pooled preference metrics [18, 19, 20] hid the per-prompt cancellation (O6), so
there was no measured symptom to chase; the triple constraint of diversity lift + fidelity
no-regression + exact collapse to the global-f base was therefore never co-targeted. ATTUNE
differs by satisfying all four clauses at once: a monotone, partially-pooled map of an
observable leakage-free covariate, at the divergence-selection layer, with a provable
E_collapse <= 1e-6 reduction to the incumbent.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method. ATTUNE (per-prompt divergence ATTUNEment) promotes the global divergence selector
alpha_global to a per-prompt selector:

    alpha(d_c) = clamp_[alpha_min, alpha_max]( alpha_global + g_theta(d_c) ),

with g_theta a monotone non-decreasing map parameterized as a sum of monotone basis functions
with no constant term (a low-dimensional monotone spline or an isotonic-constrained MLP), so that
theta = 0 makes the whole map identically zero (g_0(d) = 0 for all d) and the precondition
alpha_global in [alpha_min, alpha_max] keeps the clamp inactive at the default. Monotonicity
encodes the hypothesis that higher diversity demand -> more mass-covering divergence (more
diversity preserved). The per-prompt covariate d_c is the normalized reference-model sample
spread of Section 2.3 (DINOv2 [28] mean pairwise distance or Vendi score [27]; text-specificity
fallback), estimated from K reference samples per prompt (default K = 16). The divergence family
is the
f-divergence DPO objective of [14]: the implicit reward becomes
r(c, x) = beta * f_alpha'( pi(x|c) / pi_ref(x|c) ) + const, where f_alpha is the chosen member
of the alpha-family (reverse-KL: f(u) = u log u; forward-KL: f(u) = -log u; Jensen-Shannon and
intermediate alpha interpolate), and the loss is the Diffusion-DPO ELBO bound [11] instantiated
with f_alpha indexed by alpha(d_c). Partial pooling: prompts are grouped into K diversity-demand
bins and the map is fit with hierarchical empirical-Bayes shrinkage [31, 32], so data-poor bins
shrink toward alpha_global (a single shared functional form across bins; the DEPLOYABLE DEFAULT
is one shared monotone map, with finer pooling optional for stability). EXACT COLLAPSE: at
theta = 0 the whole map g_0 is identically zero, so (with alpha_global inside the clamp range)
alpha(d_c) = alpha_global for every c, f_alpha is identical across prompts, and L_ATTUNE == L_base
term for term; E_collapse = max_c | L_ATTUNE(c) - L_base(c) | <= 1e-6, asserted as a CI unit test.
Setting alpha_global to reverse-KL further collapses to Diffusion-DPO [11].

Datasets. Training preference data: Pick-a-Pic v2 [19] (851K pairwise human preferences) and
HPDv2 [20]; ImageReward's preference data [18] as a transfer check. Base models: Stable
Diffusion 1.5 [1] and SDXL [2] (the Diffusion-DPO setting [11]). Evaluation prompts,
diversity-demand-stratified into deciles by d_c: PartiPrompts [29], DrawBench (from Imagen [3]),
Pick-a-Pic v2 test prompts [19], and the HPSv2 benchmark prompts [20]; the top decile
(open-ended prompts) is the PRIMARY slice and a constrained-prompt subset is the fidelity-guard
slice. Semi-synthetic control: prompt sets with KNOWN diversity demand, built by mixing
template-constrained prompts (low demand) with open prompts (high demand) and sweeping the
demand spread, giving a ground-truth monotone-dose axis.

Baselines. B1 base SD/SDXL, no alignment [1, 2]; B2 Diffusion-DPO, global reverse-KL [11]
(collapse sub-target); B3 single global best f-divergence DPO (alpha_global tuned on pooled
validation), following [14] on the diffusion ELBO [11] = PRIMARY collapse target; B4 unpooled
free per-prompt alpha (tests the necessity of partial pooling, expected to overfit under O2);
B5 covariate-scrambled alpha (shuffle d_c across prompts) = placebo isolating the covariate;
B6 per-prompt oracle alpha (grid-searched per prompt against held-out diversity+fidelity) =
impractical upper bound; B7 beta-DPO [23] (adapts strength not type, controls for "any
adaptivity helps"); B8 ATTUNE (ours).

Metrics. PRIMARY: in-prompt generation diversity on the top diversity-demand decile -- mean
pairwise DINOv2 [28] feature distance and Vendi score [27] over N samples per prompt (default
N = 32) -- SUBJECT TO no regression in fidelity/faithfulness (PickScore [19], ImageReward [18],
HPSv2 [20], CLIPScore, and per-prompt precision [26]; FID [30] is reported only on
COCO-distributed prompts, where a COCO reference is meaningful, and not on PartiPrompts/DrawBench)
and no regression in pooled preference win-rate vs B2/B3, where "no regression" is pre-registered
as within the bootstrap 95% confidence interval or 1% relative, whichever is larger. SECONDARY:
diversity-demand-stratified slope (diversity gain vs d_c decile, expected positive and monotone),
generative precision/recall [26] (recall = coverage/diversity, precision = fidelity), the
constrained-prompt fidelity-guard slice (must not regress), covariate calibration (does
alpha(d_c) track the oracle alpha*(c) from B6?), the pooled no-regression band, E_collapse <=
1e-6, and compute parity (zero added inference cost). Human evaluation: side-by-side diversity
and fidelity judgments on the top-decile prompts.

Ablations. A1 covariate choice (DINOv2 spread vs Vendi vs CLIP-text specificity vs prompt
length); A2 map family and monotonicity on/off; A3 pooling (unpooled = B4, fully pooled = B3,
partial with K bins) and shrinkage strength; A4 divergence parameterization (alpha-family vs an
explicit {reverse-KL, Jensen-Shannon, forward-KL} mixture); A5 number of reference samples K used
to estimate d_c (cost vs accuracy, and reuse of training samples); A6 clamp range
[alpha_min, alpha_max]; A7 base model (SD1.5 vs SDXL) and preference dataset (Pick-a-Pic vs HPDv2).

Falsifiability tests. T1 homogeneous-prompt null: on a prompt set with near-constant d_c, ATTUNE
reduces to SOME single global divergence and therefore must NOT outperform a single-global-f
baseline RETUNED on that same homogeneous set (B3 retuned); any advantage there is an artifact,
not the per-prompt mechanism (this null is separate from the exact-identity guard
E_collapse <= 1e-6, which holds only at theta = 0). T2 monotone-dose: as the semi-synthetic diversity-demand spread increases, ATTUNE's lift over B3
must increase monotonically -- non-monotonicity refutes the mechanism. T3 covariate-scramble:
shuffling d_c (B5) must make the lift vanish to B3 level -- surviving lift means the covariate is
not what is doing the work.

Expected outcomes (hypotheses; all numeric ranges are [conjectured, no preliminary results]).
H1: ATTUNE lifts top-decile in-prompt diversity (DINOv2/Vendi) by a [conjectured] margin over B3
with no FID/PickScore/ImageReward/HPSv2 regression beyond a [conjectured] small band. H2: the
diversity-demand-stratified slope is positive and monotone, with the lift concentrated in
high-d_c prompts [conjectured]. H3: ATTUNE does not regress pooled preference win-rate vs B2/B3
(within a [conjectured] small band). H4: ATTUNE dominates B4 (unpooled) and B7 (beta-DPO) on the
diversity-fidelity Pareto frontier and approaches B6 (oracle) [conjectured]. H5: T1/T2/T3 behave
as predicted (homogeneous null shows no advantage over a retuned global-f baseline, dose is
monotone, scramble nullifies the lift) [conjectured]. H6 (not
conjectured, verifiable): E_collapse <= 1e-6 holds by construction at theta = 0, and
alpha_global = reverse-KL further collapses ATTUNE to Diffusion-DPO [11].

Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations. F1 safety-bounded diversity: increasing
mass-covering must not amplify unsafe, biased, or policy-violating content; retain the base
safety/NSFW filter, measure diversity ONLY within safe content, add a safety-rate guard that
must not regress, and never increase the diversity of harmful concepts. F2 demographic
representation vs stereotyping: for person-generating prompts, diversity intersects with
demographic representation; audit that any lift improves demographic coverage rather than
amplifying stereotypes, verify that d_c does not become a proxy for sensitive attributes that
systematically under- or over-aligns sensitive prompts, and report diversity disaggregated by
prompt category. F3 do-no-harm collapse default: theta = 0 is an exact disable switch
(E_collapse <= 1e-6) recovering the deployed base method, so ATTUNE is shadow-deployable and any
active configuration is validation-gated; ship with the switch. F4 scientific-integrity: all
numeric hypotheses are marked [conjectured, no preliminary results], and the E_collapse unit
test and the semi-synthetic demand-sweep generators are released so the mechanism and the
collapse identity are independently reproducible. F5 auditability/governance: log per-prompt
alpha(d_c) and d_c, document the covariate estimator and pooling, subject the diversity-demand
covariate to governance review (it must reflect prompt open-endedness, not a sensitive-attribute
proxy), and require human review for high-stakes prompt categories.
